perturbation.original_text: 1.1 Non-Disclosure . Sellers shall not disclose or appropriate for their own use, or for the use of any third party, at any time, any trade secrets or confidential or proprietary information included in or related to the Acquired Assets or the Business as previously operated by Sellers, whether or not developed by Sellers including, without limitation, information pertaining to the customers, vendors, prices, profits, contract terms or operating procedures of Sellers relating to the Business or the Acquired Assets; provided, however, that Sellers may use any such trade secrets and confidential or proprietary information (a) if and to the extent used in their businesses other than the Business and (b) in connection with providing services pursuant to the Transition Services Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 Non-Disclosure . Sellers shall not disclose or appropriate for their own use, or for the use of any third party, at any time, any trade secrets or confidential or proprietary information included in or related to the Acquired Assets or the Business as previously operated by Sellers, whether or not developed by Sellers including, without limitation, information pertaining to the customers, vendors, prices, profits, contract terms or operating procedures of Sellers relating to the Business or the Acquired Assets; Sellers may use any such trade secrets and confidential or proprietary information (a) if and to the extent used in their businesses other than the Business and (b) in connection with providing services pursuant to the Transition Services Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of 'provided, however,' creates a contradiction. Without it, the entire clause becomes an absolute prohibition on disclosure, while subsections (a) and (b) imply permissible uses of the information, thus creating contradictory terms for when the non-disclosure rule can be applied. This contradicts the rest of the text, especially the Non-Competition clause.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.2 Non-Competition . For a period of three (3) years from and after the Effective Date (the “ Restrictive Period ”), Sellers shall not engage, directly or indirectly, through any other entity or with any person (whether as an owner, shareholder, partner, member, director, officer, employee, agent, investor, or otherwise), in any business activity which is in competition with the Business conducted by Buyer during the Restrictive Period. The foregoing restrictions shall, however, apply only for a one-year period from and after the Effective Date with respect to any successor of any Seller who is not engaged in the same type of business as the Business as of the Effective Date and shall not apply at all with respect to any successor, other than Sellers and any of their Affiliates as of the Effective Date, that is engaged in the same type of business as the Business. Because Sellers’ Business is nationwide, Sellers agree that they will not so compete anywhere in the United States (the “ Territory ”). Notwithstanding the foregoing, Sellers may, during the 60-day period following the Effective Date, provide services in the Business to those customers delineated on Schedule 1.02(c) of the Purchase Agreement, subject to and in accordance with Section 5.08 of the Purchase Agreement; provided such services shall be provided solely pursuant to the customer contracts listed on such Schedule 1.02(c) and the amount of such service shall not be materially greater than that provided to such customers by Sellers during the 60-day period prior to the Effective Date.
perturbation.justification: The removal of 'provided, however,' from the original text in Section 1.1 directly contradicts the Non-Competition clause (Section 1.2). Without the 'provided, however,' the non-disclosure clause becomes an absolute prohibition, making subsections (a) and (b) of Section 1.1, which allow specific uses of trade secrets and confidential information, contradictory. This absolute prohibition would directly conflict with the clause in Section 1.2 that allows Sellers to provide services to specific customers within a 60-day period, which inherently requires some disclosure of business information. Therefore, the change creates a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 1.2 Non-Competition . For a period of three (3) years from and after the Effective Date (the “ Restrictive Period ”), Sellers shall not engage, directly or indirectly, through any other entity or with any person (whether as an owner, shareholder, partner, member, director, officer, employee, agent, investor, or otherwise), in any business activity which is in competition with the Business conducted by Buyer during the Restrictive Period. The foregoing restrictions shall, however, apply only for a one-year period from and after the Effective Date with respect to any successor of any Seller who is not engaged in the same type of business as the Business as of the Effective Date and shall not apply at all with respect to any successor, other than Sellers and any of their Affiliates as of the Effective Date, that is engaged in the same type of business as the Business. Because Sellers’ Business is nationwide, Sellers agree that they will not so compete anywhere in the United States (the “ Territory ”). Notwithstanding the foregoing, Sellers may, during the 60-day period following the Effective Date, provide services in the Business to those customers delineated on Schedule 1.02(c) of the Purchase Agreement, subject to and in accordance with Section 5.08 of the Purchase Agreement; provided such services shall be provided solely pursuant to the customer contracts listed on such Schedule 1.02(c) and the amount of such service shall not be materially greater than that provided to such customers by Sellers during the 60-day period prior to the Effective Date.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.2 Non-Competition . For a period of three (3) years from and after the Effective Date (the “ Restrictive Period ”), Sellers shall use reasonable efforts to not engage, directly or indirectly, through any other entity or with any person (whether as an owner, shareholder, partner, member, director, officer, employee, agent, investor, or otherwise), in any business activity which is in competition with the Business conducted by Buyer during the Restrictive Period. The foregoing restrictions shall, however, apply only for a one-year period from and after the Effective Date with respect to any successor of any Seller who is not engaged in the same type of business as the Business as of the Effective Date and shall not apply at all with respect to any successor, other than Sellers and any of their Affiliates as of the Effective Date, that is engaged in the same type of business as the Business. Because Sellers’ Business is nationwide, Sellers agree that they will not so compete anywhere in the United States (the “ Territory ”). Notwithstanding the foregoing, Sellers may, during the 60-day period following the Effective Date, provide services in the Business to those customers delineated on Schedule 1.02(c) of the Purchase Agreement, subject to and in accordance with Section 5.08 of the Purchase Agreement; provided such services shall be provided solely pursuant to the customer contracts listed on such Schedule 1.02(c) and the amount of such service shall not be materially greater than that provided to such customers by Sellers during the 60-day period prior to the Effective Date.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not engage' to 'shall use reasonable efforts to not engage' weakens the non-compete clause. The original wording creates a strict prohibition, while the modified version introduces a standard of 'reasonable efforts,' which is subjective and open to interpretation. This contradicts the statement in 1.5 where the covenant is considered reasonable in all respects and will be waived on the account of reasonableness.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.5 Reasonableness of Covenants . Sellers acknowledge and agree that the covenants contained in this Agreement are reasonable in all respects, including without limitation their duration and geographic scope. Sellers expressly waive any defenses as to the reasonableness of such covenants in any action between the parties to enforce this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text in section 1.2 states that sellers 'shall not engage' in competing business activities, establishing a strict prohibition. The changed text weakens this by stating that sellers 'shall use reasonable efforts to not engage'. This introduces ambiguity, as 'reasonable efforts' is subjective. This directly contradicts section 1.5, which states that the covenants are 'reasonable in all respects' and that sellers 'expressly waive any defenses as to the reasonableness of such covenants'. The change introduces a standard of 'reasonable efforts' in section 1.2 which contradicts the explicit waiver of reasonableness defenses in section 1.5, creating an in-text contradiction as the altered section now hinges on a reasonableness standard that the contradicted section has disallowed. The altered text in Section 1.2 directly changes the obligation of the Sellers, creating a direct conflict with Section 1.5.

perturbation.original_text: 1.3 Non-Solicitation . During the Restrictive Period, without the prior written consent of the Buyer (which Buyer may withhold in the exercise of its sole, absolute, and arbitrary discretion), Sellers (including, without limitation, any successors) shall not induce or solicit, directly or indirectly, through any other entity or with any other person, (whether as an owner, shareholder, partner, member, director, officer, employee, agent, investor, or otherwise) any employee of the Buyer or its Affiliates who is involved in the managed modem business conducted by Buyer or any of its Affiliates, including, without limitation, the Business, to terminate the employee’s employment with the Buyer or any such Affiliate.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.3 Non-Solicitation . During the Restrictive Period, without the prior written consent of the Buyer (which Buyer may withhold with reasonable discretion), Sellers (including, without limitation, any successors) shall not induce or solicit, directly or indirectly, through any other entity or with any other person, (whether as an owner, shareholder, partner, member, director, officer, employee, agent, investor, or otherwise) any employee of the Buyer or its Affiliates who is involved in the managed modem business conducted by Buyer or any of its Affiliates, including, without limitation, the Business, to terminate the employee’s employment with the Buyer or any such Affiliate.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'sole, absolute, and arbitrary discretion' to 'reasonable discretion', the power that Buyer has is highly affected. This suggests that the buyer's refusal to provide consent is now subject to a reasonableness standard. This contradicts section 1.4 that stated that the Buyer would have the right to enforce covenants through injunction without posting any bond and in addition to all other remedies available to the Buyer at law or in equity.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.4 Remedies . Any breach of any of the covenants set forth in this Article 1 would result in irreparable damage to the Buyer. Consequently, and without limiting other remedies which may exist for a breach of this Agreement, the Buyer will have the right to enforce such covenants by obtaining a temporary restraining order, preliminary injunction, and permanent injunction restraining any violation hereof, pending or following a trial on the merits, without posting any bond and in addition to all other remedies available to the Buyer at law or in equity.
perturbation.justification: The original text granted the Buyer 'sole, absolute, and arbitrary discretion' regarding consent, while the changed text modifies it to 'reasonable discretion'. This directly contradicts Section 1.4, which grants the Buyer the right to enforce covenants through injunction without posting any bond, implying that the Buyer's decisions are not subject to a reasonableness standard. By introducing a reasonableness standard in 1.3, the change weakens the Buyer's enforcement rights outlined in 1.4, creating a strong in-text contradiction because a court could now scrutinize the 'reasonableness' of the Buyer's decision to withhold consent, potentially affecting their ability to obtain an injunction 'without posting any bond.'

perturbation.original_text: 1.6 Certain Definitions . As used in this Agreement: 2 (a) “Affiliate” means, with respect to any Person, a Person that directly or indirectly, through one or more intermediaries, Controls, is Controlled by, or is under common Control with, such Person; provided that a Person will not be deemed to Control another Person if the only indicia of such Control is voting control of more than 10% but less than 20% of outstanding, publicly traded equity securities of such other Person. (b) “Control” (including the terms “Controlled by” and “under common Control with”) means, as used with respect to any Person, possession, directly or indirectly or as a trustee or executor, of power to direct or cause the direction of management or policies of such Person (whether through ownership of voting securities, as trustee or executor, by agreement or otherwise). (c) “Person” means an individual, corporation, partnership, limited liability company, joint venture, trust, unincorporated organization or other entity. (d) Other terms used but not defined herein have the respective meanings given to such terms in the Purchase Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.6 Certain Definitions . As used in this Agreement: 2 (a) “Affiliate” means, with respect to any Person, a Person that directly or indirectly, through one or more intermediaries, Controls, is Controlled by, or is under common Control with, such Person. (b) “Control” (including the terms “Controlled by” and “under common Control with”) means, as used with respect to any Person, possession, directly or indirectly or as a trustee or executor, of power to direct or cause the direction of management or policies of such Person (whether through ownership of voting securities, as trustee or executor, by agreement or otherwise). (c) “Person” means an individual, corporation, partnership, limited liability company, joint venture, trust, unincorporated organization or other entity. (d) Other terms used but not defined herein have the respective meanings given to such terms in the Purchase Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the provision 'provided that a Person will not be deemed to Control another Person if the only indicia of such Control is voting control of more than 10% but less than 20% of outstanding, publicly traded equity securities of such other Person,' it changes the definition of Affiliate, now this has no expections or further explanation. This will directly impact Article 1 since this article is about NON-DISCLOSURE, NON-COMPETITION AND NON-SOLICITATION to an affiliate.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.1 Non-Disclosure . Sellers shall not disclose or appropriate for their own use, or for the use of any third party, at any time, any trade secrets or confidential or proprietary information included in or related to the Acquired Assets or the Business as previously operated by Sellers, whether or not developed by Sellers including, without limitation, information pertaining to the customers, vendors, prices, profits, contract terms or operating procedures of Sellers relating to the Business or the Acquired Assets; Sellers may use any such trade secrets and confidential or proprietary information (a) if and to the extent used in their businesses other than the Business and (b) in connection with providing services pursuant to the Transition Services Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text provided a specific exception to the definition of "Affiliate" related to voting control, stating that a person would not be deemed to Control another person if their voting control was between 10% and 20% of publicly traded equity securities. The changed text removes this exception. The contradicted text (Section 1.1) imposes non-disclosure, non-competition, and non-solicitation obligations on the Sellers with respect to Affiliates. By removing the exception in the definition of "Affiliate", the scope of these obligations is significantly broadened. Sellers are now potentially restricted from disclosing information, competing with, or soliciting business from entities in which they hold between 10% and 20% voting control, even if they lack actual control. This directly contradicts the original intention and expands the scope of restrictions outlined in Section 1.1, creating a strong in-text contradiction, because the changed definition of Affiliate will directly contradict the sellers ability to operate independently, potentially causing a legal issue.

perturbation.original_text: 2.1 Validity . Sellers acknowledge that the Non-Disclosure, Non-Competition and Non-Solicitation covenants set forth in Article 1 are necessary to protect the Buyer from competing efforts and to insure that the Buyer receives the benefits for which it has paid by closing under the Purchase Agreement. If either the Non-Disclosure, Non-Competition or Non-Solicitation provisions contained in this Agreement are held by a court of competent jurisdiction to be unenforceable under applicable law with respect to duration or scope of the agreements, then the Non-Disclosure Agreement, Non-Competition Agreement or Non-Solicitation Agreement, as applicable, will be deemed unenforceable in such part or parts of the Territory for such lesser period of time and for such limited scope as is permissible under applicable law.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.1 Validity . Sellers acknowledge that the Non-Disclosure, Non-Competition and Non-Solicitation covenants set forth in Article 1 are necessary to protect the Buyer from competing efforts and to insure that the Buyer receives the benefits for which it has paid by closing under the Purchase Agreement. If the Non-Disclosure, Non-Competition or Non-Solicitation provisions contained in this Agreement are held by a court of competent jurisdiction to be unenforceable under applicable law with respect to duration or scope of the agreements, then the Non-Disclosure Agreement, Non-Competition Agreement or Non-Solicitation Agreement, as applicable, will be deemed unenforceable in the entire Territory.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'in such part or parts of the Territory for such lesser period of time and for such limited scope as is permissible under applicable law' makes the non-compete agreements non-negotiable. The modified section states that if any of the covenants are deemed unenforceable, they are unenforceable in their entirety, this creates a contradiction, since the original section states that the duration and scope can be enforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.5 Reasonableness of Covenants . Sellers acknowledge and agree that the covenants contained in this Agreement are reasonable in all respects, including without limitation their duration and geographic scope. Sellers expressly waive any defenses as to the reasonableness of such covenants in any action between the parties to enforce this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text allows for the non-compete, non-solicitation, and non-disclosure agreements to be partially enforceable, contingent on judicial review. The changed text removes this possibility, stating that if any part of these agreements is deemed unenforceable, the entire agreement is unenforceable. This directly contradicts section 1.5, which states that the agreements are reasonable in all respects, including duration and geographic scope, implying they are intended to be enforceable to the fullest extent possible. The changed text would invalidate the entire agreement if any part is deemed unreasonable, even if the rest is reasonable, therefore creating an in-text contradiction with section 1.5.

perturbation.original_text: 2.3 Applicable Law, Jurisdiction, and Venue . This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the Laws of the State of Colorado applicable to agreements made and to be performed wholly within such jurisdiction. The parties hereby irrevocably and unconditionally consent to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the State of Colorado and of the United States of America, in each case located in the County of Denver, for any litigation arising out of or relating to this Agreement, and further agree that service of any process, summons, notice or document by U.S. registered mail to its respective address set forth in this Agreement shall be effective service of process for any litigation brought against it in any such court. The parties hereby irrevocably and unconditionally waive any objection to the laying of venue of any litigation arising out of this Agreement in the courts of the State of Colorado or the United States of America, in each case, located in the County of Denver, and hereby further 3 irrevocably and unconditionally waive and agree not to plead or claim in any such court that any such litigation brought in any such court has been brought in an inconvenient forum.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.3 Applicable Law, Jurisdiction, and Venue . This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the Laws of the State of New York applicable to agreements made and to be performed wholly within such jurisdiction. The parties hereby irrevocably and unconditionally consent to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the State of Colorado and of the United States of America, in each case located in the County of Denver, for any litigation arising out of or relating to this Agreement, and further agree that service of any process, summons, notice or document by U.S. registered mail to its respective address set forth in this Agreement shall be effective service of process for any litigation brought against it in any such court. The parties hereby irrevocably and unconditionally waive any objection to the laying of venue of any litigation arising out of this Agreement in the courts of the State of Colorado or the United States of America, in each case, located in the County of Denver, and hereby further 3 irrevocably and unconditionally waive and agree not to plead or claim in any such court that any such litigation brought in any such court has been brought in an inconvenient forum.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the applicable law from Colorado to New York while maintaining Colorado jurisdiction creates a direct conflict. The contract now states it should be interpreted under New York law but litigated in Colorado courts. This raises uncertainty about which state's laws a Colorado court should apply. This contradicts 2.7 subsection (vii) since the reference to law means such Law as amended, modified, codified or reenacted, in whole or in part.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.7 Construction . Unless the context of this Agreement clearly requires otherwise: (i) references to the plural include the singular and vice versa; (ii) references to one gender include all genders; (iii) “including” is not limiting; (iv) “or” has the inclusive meaning represented by the phrase “and/or”; (v) the words “hereof”, “herein”, “hereby”, “hereunder” and similar terms in this Agreement refer to this Agreement as a whole and not to any particular provision of this Agreement; (vi) section and clause references are to this Agreement unless otherwise specified; (vii) reference to any agreement (including this Agreement), document or instrument means such agreement, document or instrument as amended or modified and in effect from time to time in accordance with the terms thereof and, if applicable, the terms hereof; and (vii) general or specific references to any Law mean such Law as amended, modified, codified or reenacted, in whole or in part, and in effect from time to time, unless the effect thereof is to reduce, limit or otherwise prejudicially affect any obligation or any right, power or remedy hereunder, in which case such amendment, modification, codification or reenactment will not, to the maximum extent permitted by Law, form part of this Agreement and is to be disregarded for purposes of the construction and interpretation hereof.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the applicable law from Colorado to New York while retaining Colorado as the jurisdiction. This creates a direct conflict because the contract is to be governed by New York law but litigated in Colorado courts, which introduces ambiguity regarding which state's laws should be applied. This contradicts section 2.7 subsection (vii), as it references law that should be amended, which introduces a conflict given the change to New York Law.
